texto: A related notion is that when you really know a language, you think in it. In fact, the brain doesn't think in any language. What people refer to as "thinking in a language" comes from being able to speak more immediately in a language without rehearsing it or translating it from a language one might know better; the spoken thought feels as if it's closer to its source in the brain.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Michael Erard: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de temas ligados a reflexao. O trecho “A related notion is that when you really know a language, you think in it. In fact, the brain doe…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que reflexao, language, think pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de dia-chuvoso e reflexao.

frase_original: A related notion is that when you really know a language, you think in it. In fact, the brain doesn't think in any language. What people refer to as "thinking in a language" comes from being able to speak more immediately in a language without rehearsing it or translating it from a language one might know better; the spoken thought feels as if it's closer to its source in the brain.

texto: Possible explanations for talented language learning fall into two general areas. One view says: What matters is a person's sense of mission and dedication to language learning. You don't need to describe high performers as biologically exceptional, because what they do is a product of practice. Anyone can become a foreign-language expert - even an adult. (...) The other view says: Something neurological is going on. We may not know exactly what the mechanisms are, but we can't explain exceptional outcomes fully through training or motivation.
explicacao: Citação de Michael Erard sobre limites e escolhas conscientes. “Possible explanations for talented language learning fall into two general areas. One view says: …” articula motivacao, language, linguistics em registro de motivacao, com relevância para contextos como dia-chuvoso e linguistics.

The other view says: Something neurological is going on. We may not know exactly what the mechanisms are, but we can't explain exceptional outcomes fully through training or motivation.

texto: What you see and hear is a situation in which languages are less like apples — neat and discrete — and more like oatmeal. It's always been oatmeal in India, and all the varieties of oatmeal continue to merge, despite political pressures to name them as if they were marbles.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Michael Erard: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de temas ligados a reflexao. O trecho “What you see and hear is a situation in which languages are less like apples — neat and discrete …” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que reflexao, india, language pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de language e multilingualism.

texto: Why are there more male hyperpolyglots? One answer is that speaking a lot of languages is a geek macho thing (...) It seemed that a woman is less likely to say she "speaks" or "knows" a language if she studied it at some point in the past, while a man, wanting to display his giant repertoire, would include it.
explicacao: Pergunta retórica de Michael Erard que provoca temas ligados a reflexao. Ao questionar “Why are there more male hyperpolyglots? One answer is that speaking a lot of languages is a geek …”, o texto convida o leitor a revisar crenças sobre reflexao, language, polyglots, com foco em polyglots.

texto: Some studies of successful language learners have suggested that they're more "open to new experiences" than the rest of us. Temptingly, psychologist Alexander Guiora proposed that we have a self that's bound up in our native language, a "language ego", which needs to be loose and more permeable to learn a new language. Those with more fluid ego boundaries, like children and people who have drunk some alcohol, are more willing to sound not like themselves, which means they have better accents in the new language.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Michael Erard: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de esforço, persistência e resultados. O trecho “Some studies of successful language learners have suggested that they're more "open to new experi…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que sabedoria, accents, language pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de language e language-learning.
